meta.title: Percentage Calculator — 4 Modes, Free & Instant
meta.description: Four percentage calculators in one: find % of a number, calculate % change, determine what % X is of Y, or adjust a value by any percentage. Free.

validation.a_required: Please enter a value for X.
validation.b_required: Please enter a value for Y.
validation.invalid_number: Please enter a valid number (e.g. 42 or 3.14).

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.q1: How do I calculate what percentage one number is of another?
faq.a1: Use the 'X is what % of Y' mode. The formula is: (X ÷ Y) × 100. For example, if 45 students passed out of 60 enrolled, select this mode, set X=45, Y=60. Result: (45/60) × 100 = 75%. This mode is used for calculating test scores, market share, completion rates, budget utilization, and any scenario where you need to express part-to-whole relationship as a percentage. If your answer seems counterintuitive, check which number is the 'part' (X) and which is the 'whole' (Y).
faq.q2: How do I calculate percentage change between two values?
faq.a2: Use the '% change from X to Y' mode. The formula is: ((Y − X) ÷ X) × 100. Where X is the starting (original) value and Y is the ending (new) value. A positive result is an increase; negative is a decrease. Example: Sales went from $80,000 to $96,000. Change = ((96,000 − 80,000) / 80,000) × 100 = 20% increase. Always use the original value (X) as the denominator — using the new value as denominator is a common error that produces an incorrect result.
faq.q3: What is the difference between percentage increase and percentage point increase?
faq.a3: This distinction matters enormously in finance, statistics, and public discourse. A 'percentage point' change is the arithmetic difference between two percentages. A 'percentage increase' is the relative change. Example: an interest rate rises from 4% to 6%. The percentage point change is 2 (6 minus 4). But the percentage increase is 50% — because 6 is 50% more than 4. Politicians and media often confuse these intentionally or unintentionally. When someone says 'crime increased by 2%' vs. 'crime increased by 2 percentage points,' the meaning and magnitude differ significantly.
faq.q4: How do I calculate percentage of a total (like budget allocation)?
faq.a4: Use the 'X is what % of Y' mode where Y is the total budget and X is the category amount. If your total budget is $15,000 and you spend $3,750 on marketing: (3,750 ÷ 15,000) × 100 = 25%. Conversely, to find how much money 25% of a $15,000 budget is, use 'What is X% of Y': X=25, Y=15,000, result = $3,750. These two modes are inverse operations and cross-verify each other.
faq.q5: How do I calculate a percentage increase to hit a target?
faq.a5: Use the '% change from X to Y' mode. Set X as the current value and Y as your target. If revenue is $120,000 and your target is $144,000: ((144,000 − 120,000) / 120,000) × 100 = 20%. You need a 20% increase. Alternatively, to find what value results from a specific increase: use 'Increase X by Y%' mode. Set X=120,000 and Y=20 to confirm: 120,000 × 1.20 = $144,000.
faq.q6: What is a common mistake when calculating percentage decrease?
faq.a6: The most common error is using the wrong denominator. Percentage decrease from 100 to 80 is NOT 20% of 80 — it is 20% of 100. The formula always divides by the original (starting) value: ((Original − New) / Original) × 100. (100 − 80) / 100 = 20%. Using 80 as the denominator gives 25%, which is wrong. Another common mistake is confusing percentage decrease with the remaining percentage: a 20% decrease leaves 80% remaining — these are different quantities representing different things.
faq.q7: How are percentages used in financial calculations?
faq.a7: Percentages are the fundamental language of finance. Interest rates (4.5% APY), investment returns (S&P 500 up 12.3% year-to-date), inflation (CPI rose 3.1%), tax rates (22% federal bracket), gross margin (revenue minus COGS divided by revenue × 100), discount rates (NPV calculations), and loan terms are all expressed as percentages. The four modes in this calculator cover every routine financial percentage calculation: finding a percentage of a dollar amount, determining what percentage one amount is of another, calculating year-over-year change, and adjusting a value up or down by a percentage.
faq.q8: How do I calculate tip percentage at a restaurant?
faq.a8: Two approaches: To find the tip amount for a specific percentage, use 'What is X% of Y' — set X=18 (for 18% tip) and Y=85 (the bill amount). Result: $15.30 tip, total = $100.30. To find what percentage a specific tip amount represents, use 'X is what % of Y' — if you left $20 on an $85 bill: (20/85) × 100 = 23.5% tip. Standard tip percentages: 15% (minimal), 18% (adequate service), 20% (good service), 25%+ (excellent service or high-end dining).

aria.form_description: Select a calculation mode and enter values to compute a percentage.

content.whatIs:
Yüzde hesaplayıcı, tek bir arayüzde dört temel yüzde problemi türünü çözer: Y'nin X%'i nedir (bir miktarın yüzdesini bulma), X, Y'nin yüzde kaçıdır (bir oranı yüzde olarak ifade etme), X'ten Y'ye yüzde değişim (iki değer arasındaki büyüme veya düşüşü ölçme) ve X'i Y% artır veya azalt (bir temel değere yüzdelik düzeltme uygulama). Bu dört işlem, günlük yaşamda, okulda, işte ve finansta karşılaşılan neredeyse her yüzde hesabını kapsamaktadır.

Yüzdeler, en evrensel kullanılan matematiksel kavramlardan biridir — ve en sık yanlış hesaplananlardan biri. Sayısal okuryazarlık araştırmaları, yüzde hesaplama hatalarının yetişkinler tarafından yapılan en sık aritmetik hatalar arasında yer aldığını göstermektedir; üniversite mezunu profesyoneller dahil. Karışıklık genellikle iki kaynaktan kaynaklanır: bir hesaplamada hangi sayının «taban» (payda) olduğu konusundaki belirsizlik ve yüzde puanlarını yüzde değişimiyle karıştırma — finansal, bilimsel ve istatistiksel bağlamlarda büyük önem taşıyan bir ayrım.

Bu hesaplayıcı, her senaryoyu belirli giriş yuvalarına sahip açıkça etiketlenmiş bir mod olarak sunarak belirsizliği ortadan kaldırır. Satış komisyonu hesaplıyor, bir sınav puanı belirliyor, yatırım getirilerini ölçüyor, bütçe kullanımını analiz ediyor, bahşiş hesaplıyor veya bir notu harf notuna dönüştürüyor olun, dört moddan biri doğrudan uygulanabilir — ve her birinin arkasındaki formül şeffaf ve doğrulanabilirdir.
content.formula:
Mod 1 — Y'nin X%'i nedir:
Sonuç = (X ÷ 100) × Y
Örnek: 200'ün %15'i nedir? = (15/100) × 200 = 30

Mod 2 — X, Y'nin yüzde kaçıdır:
Sonuç = (X ÷ Y) × 100
Örnek: 30, 200'ün yüzde kaçıdır? = (30/200) × 100 = %15

Mod 3 — X'ten Y'ye % değişim:
Sonuç = ((Y − X) ÷ X) × 100
Pozitif = artış, Negatif = azalış
Örnek: 80'den 96'ya → ((96−80)/80)×100 = +%20

Mod 4 — X'i Y% artır / azalt:
Artır: Sonuç = X × (1 + Y/100)
Azalt: Sonuç = X × (1 − Y/100)
Örnek: 200'ü %15 artır = 200 × 1,15 = 230
content.example:
Örnek 1 — Not yüzdesi:
Sınavda 60 üzerinden 47 aldınız.
Mod: «X, Y'nin yüzde kaçıdır». X=47, Y=60.
Sonuç: (47/60) × 100 = %78,3 — iyi bir not.

Örnek 2 — Satış komisyonu:
12.500 ₺'lik bir satışta %8 komisyon kazandınız.
Mod: «Y'nin X%'i nedir». X=8, Y=12500.
Sonuç: (8/100) × 12.500 = 1.000 ₺ komisyon.

Örnek 3 — Gelir büyümesi:
Geçen yıl gelir 340.000 ₺, bu yıl 408.000 ₺.
Mod: «X'ten Y'ye % değişim». X=340000, Y=408000.
Sonuç: ((408.000 − 340.000) / 340.000) × 100 = +%20 büyüme.

Örnek 4 — Zam sonrası maaş:
Mevcut maaş 52.000 ₺. %6,5 zam aldınız.
Mod: «X'i Y% artır». X=52000, Y=6,5.
Sonuç: 52.000 ₺ × 1,065 = 55.380 ₺ yeni maaş.
Zam tutarı: 55.380 ₺ − 52.000 ₺ = 3.380 ₺.
content.howItWorks_1: Açılır menüden sorunuza uyan hesaplama modunu seçin: «Y'nin X%'i nedir» (bir sayının yüzdesini bulma), «X, Y'nin yüzde kaçıdır» (bir oranı yüzde olarak ifade etme), «X'ten Y'ye % değişim» (büyüme veya düşüşü ölçme) veya «X'i Y% artır/azalt» (yüzdelik düzeltme uygulama). Her mod ayrı bir matematiksel soruyu çözer.
content.howItWorks_2: X ve Y alanlarına iki değeri girin. Etiketler, seçilen modunuza göre hangi sayının nereye gittiğini netleştirmek için güncellenir — yüzde hesaplama hatalarının en yaygın kaynağını ortadan kaldırır. Ayarlama modu için yön geçişinden Artır veya Azalt'ı seçin.
content.howItWorks_3: Hesapla'ya tıklayın. Sonuç, iki ondalık basamağa yuvarlanmış sayısal cevapla anında görünür. Sonuç etiketi, sayının neyi temsil ettiğini onaylar — yüzde, para tutarı, nihai değer — bu nedenle çıktının özel sorunuzun bağlamında ne anlama geldiği konusunda hiçbir belirsizlik yoktur.
content.whyItMatters:
Percentage calculations appear in virtually every domain of daily decision-making — from reading a nutrition label (20% daily value of sodium) to evaluating a salary offer (5% annual raise), from understanding election poll margins (3% point lead) to calculating whether a sale is worth making a trip to the store (30% off a $40 item saves $12, not always worth driving 20 minutes). The ability to perform these calculations quickly and correctly is a foundational component of mathematical literacy that has real financial consequences.

In finance specifically, the four percentage modes map directly to routine tasks. Mode 1 (percent of a number) calculates taxes owed, tip amounts, investment allocations, and interest on a balance. Mode 2 (what percent is X of Y) calculates profit margins, passing rates, budget utilization, and market share. Mode 3 (percentage change) calculates year-over-year revenue growth, inflation rates, portfolio returns, and price changes. Mode 4 (percentage adjustment) calculates prices after raises, costs after markups, values after depreciation, and returns after fees.

The distinction between percentage points and percentage change — which this calculator's % change mode handles correctly — is particularly important for financial and statistical literacy. When interest rates rise from 2% to 3%, they rise by 1 percentage point but by 50% in relative terms. When a politician claims a policy 'reduced unemployment by 2%,' that could mean unemployment fell from 10% to 8% (a 2 percentage point drop) or from 10% to 9.8% (a 2% relative reduction). These are wildly different outcomes. Understanding which interpretation is intended requires percentage literacy — a skill this calculator reinforces through consistent, transparent formula application.
content.limitations:
This calculator handles four standard percentage scenarios using conventional mathematical definitions. It does not handle compound percentage changes — a 10% increase followed by a 10% decrease does NOT return to the original value (it leaves you at 99%). If you apply a 10% increase to $100, you get $110. Then 10% off $110 gives $99, not $100. For sequences of percentage changes, each must be calculated separately, using the output of each step as the input to the next.

The percentage change mode uses the original (starting) value as the denominator, which is the mathematically standard definition. However, some contexts — particularly in finance — use different denominators. For example, simple interest rate of return uses initial investment as the base, while some other measures use average of start and end values. Always verify which definition is expected in your specific context before using the output in a formal report or analysis.

This calculator rounds results to two decimal places. For calculations where precision beyond two decimal places matters — scientific research, actuarial calculations, engineering tolerances — the rounding may introduce small errors when the rounded output is used as an input for subsequent calculations. For these high-precision use cases, retain more decimal places at each intermediate step.
content.tips_1: When calculating percentage change, always identify which value is the starting point (X) and which is the ending point (Y) before entering numbers. The most common error in percentage change calculations is reversing X and Y, which inverts the result. A stock that fell from $50 to $40 shows a −20% change when entered correctly (X=50, Y=40), but incorrectly entered (X=40, Y=50) gives +25% — the wrong direction and magnitude.
content.tips_2: For percentage-of calculations in budgeting or resource allocation, cross-check your result: Mode 1 and Mode 2 are inverse operations. If 15% of 200 is 30 (Mode 1), then 30 is what % of 200 should return 15% (Mode 2). Using both modes to cross-check each other takes five seconds and eliminates calculation errors entirely — especially useful before presenting budget numbers in a meeting or report.
content.tips_3: For business margin calculations, be precise about 'margin' vs 'markup.' Gross margin is profit as a percentage of revenue: (Revenue − COGS) / Revenue × 100. Markup is profit as a percentage of cost: (Revenue − COGS) / COGS × 100. A product that costs $60 and sells for $100 has a 40% gross margin but a 66.7% markup. Confusing these in pricing decisions is a common business error — always specify which measure you are using.
content.tips_4: When interpreting percentage data from news, research, or marketing, always ask: percentage of what? 'Crime rose 15%' is meaningless without knowing the base. Is it 15% of last year's numbers? 15% of the 5-year average? 15% of a single-month spike? Similarly, '30% more effective' in advertising requires a control group baseline to be meaningful. Asking 'percentage of what?' is the most powerful critical thinking tool for evaluating percentage-based claims.
